hinge: Cold lateral condensation is the gold standard obturation technique taught in board curricula for its predictable three-dimensional seal, control, and long-term clinical track record. Warm vertical condensation is also excellent but cold lateral is the classic board-standard answer.

Among hand instruments, the reamer has a triangular cross-section with fewer flutes and a looser spiral, making it more flexible than K-files. However, the barbed broach is the most fragile/flexible by design though it is used for pulp extirpation, not canal shaping.
Throbbing, dull, aching pain is mediated by slow-conducting unmyelinated C-fibers in the pulp. Sharp, brief pain is mediated by fast-conducting myelinated A-delta fibers.

A complicated crown fracture with an open apex within 3 hours should receive direct pulp capping to preserve pulp vitality and allow continued root development (apexogenesis). Pulpectomy and RCT would be reserved for cases where the pulp cannot be preserved.

For primary endo-secondary perio lesions, endodontic treatment should be completed first to eliminate the intraradicular source of infection. Periodontal therapy follows to address the secondary periodontal defect.

A tooth under a metal crown may give a false positive response to EPT if the current conducts through the metal to adjacent gingival tissue. Combined with no response to cold and radiographic findings, this indicates pulpal necrosis.

For an avulsed tooth with >60 minutes extraoral time and closed apex in an 11-year-old, PDL necrosis is inevitable. Sodium fluoride treatment before replantation is recommended to slow root resorption.

Extraoral time is overwhelmingly the most influential factor for successful reimplantation. The sooner the tooth is replanted, the higher the survival rate. Antibiotics and tetanus are secondary considerations.

Internal resorption creates an irregular, enlarged canal space that cannot be obturated with standard lateral condensation. Warm vertical compaction with thermoplasticized gutta-percha is required to flow into and fill the irregular resorptive defect three-dimensionally.

A crowned maxillary molar with an isolated deep pocket on one root (7 mm vs 3-4 mm elsewhere), no pain on percussion or palpation suggests a root fracture or isolated periodontal defect on that single root. Root amputation of the affected root is the appropriate treatment to preserve the remaining roots and crown.

A tooth with a buccal abscess, no response to cold, and no percussion pain is necrotic. The best management is non-surgical RCT to address the intraradicular infection source. Antibiotics alone do not resolve the root canal infection and are only an adjunct when systemic signs exist.
RC-Prep is a chelating lubricant containing EDTA, urea peroxide, and glycol. EDTA provides chelation, urea peroxide provides bubbling/effervescence, and glycol serves as a lubricating vehicle.

Ferric sulfate used as a pulp-capping or pulpotomy agent in primary teeth is associated with internal root resorption. The acidic hemostatic agent damages the underlying odontoblasts and pulp tissue, triggering chronic inflammation and osteoclastic activity within the root canal.

No bleeding upon entering the pulp chamber during pulpotomy indicates pulpal necrosis — the coronal pulp has undergone liquefactive necrosis and lost its blood supply. A healthy or inflamed pulp would bleed upon entry.

A primary tooth with a gingival abscess has a necrotic pulp that has progressed beyond the apex into the periodontal tissues. Antibiotics provide only temporary relief — the underlying pulpal condition is irreversible pulpitis/necrosis requiring pulpectomy or extraction.

A traumatized tooth 20 years ago with calcified canals, no symptoms, and no periapical pathology does not require treatment. If treatment becomes necessary, the prognosis for non-surgical RCT is fair (not good) due to the difficulty of locating and negotiating calcified canals with elevated risk of perforation or instrument separation.

An isolated deep pocket with a negative cold test and extensive bone loss around a tooth with minimal restoration suggests the periodontal infection has tracked to the apex, causing retrograde pulp necrosis — primary perio with secondary endo involvement.

For a painful tooth with difficult anatomy presenting to ER where RCT is not feasible, extraction is the most definitive emergency management. Pulpotomy and referral would be ideal but was explicitly excluded from options. NSAIDs provide only temporary symptomatic relief.

A 14-year-old with a pinpoint pulp exposure within 24 hours (1 day) and an open apex is best treated with Cvek partial pulpotomy, which removes only the superficial contaminated pulp (1-2 mm) and preserves the healthy radicular pulp for continued root development.

A tooth displaced palatally with associated bone fracture is a lateral luxation injury. Intrusion involves apical displacement into the socket; extrusion is displacement out of the socket; subluxation is increased mobility without displacement.

Root fractures in the cervical third have the poorest prognosis because of proximity to the oral cavity with bacterial contamination through the gingival sulcus, difficulty of splinting, and insufficient root length for coronal fragment retention.
Apical third root fractures have the best prognosis because they have the least mobility, maximal root length for healing, and the least risk of bacterial contamination from the oral cavity.

Gutta-percha extrusion past the apex most commonly results from lack of control during obturation — failure to establish an apical stop, excessive pressure, or inadequate working length determination.

When a file breaks in the apical third during endodontic treatment and cannot be bypassed or retrieved, obturating to the level of the broken file with appropriate seal is the accepted conservative management. The file becomes part of the obturation, extraction and apical surgery are more invasive alternatives.

A 14-year-old with a complicated crown fracture involving 2/3 of the crown requires pulpotomy to remove the contaminated coronal pulp and preserve the radicular pulp for continued root maturation. DPC would be insufficient for such large exposure; RCT would sacrifice pulp vitality unnecessarily.

Among endodontic hand instruments, the barbed broach is manually operated for pulp tissue removal. Gates-Glidden, lentulo spiral, and Peeso reamers are all rotary/latch-type instruments used with a handpiece.

Pain with cold drinks indicates a pulp-mediated response. The first diagnostic test should be a cold test to reproduce the patient's chief complaint and localize the symptomatic tooth. EPT tests nerve fibers but not pulp health directly.
A pink hue visible through the crown (pink tooth of Mummery) indicates internal resorption where granulation/vascular tissue within the pulp chamber shows through thin remaining dentin and enamel.

For an avulsed tooth, CBCT and multiple PAs are recommended to assess root position, alveolar bone integrity, and to rule out associated fractures before replantation. Occlusal and OPG alone provide insufficient detail.
Sodium hypochlorite has a pH of approximately 11-12, making it strongly alkaline. This high pH provides its tissue-dissolving and antimicrobial properties.
